steps.title: Pin down the key evidence in the passage
steps.content: Scientists observed 6,000 M. robustus adults, hatchlings, and eggs at the Octopus Garden over three years, but no juveniles. The site is used exclusively for reproduction. The missing life stage — juveniles — is the critical fact: something happens between "hatchling" and "adult" that takes the octopuses away from the garden.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices, confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — M. robustus leave the Octopus Garden upon reaching an intermediary stage of development.: Directly explains the absence of juveniles: octopuses depart when they reach the juvenile stage, accounting for the gap between hatchlings and adults. ✓
  • Choice B — The M. robustus population at the Octopus Garden remains stable despite variations in water temperature.: The passage never discusses population stability or describes problematic temperature variations — temperatures are presented as likely beneficial. ✗
  • Choice C — M. robustus nests in the Octopus Garden contain on average fewer but larger eggs than nests at similar ocean depths.: The passage mentions eggs are present but provides no data on egg size or number, and makes no comparison to other ocean depths. ✗
  • Choice D — The Octopus Garden provides an ideal feeding ground for M. robustus hatchlings.: The passage states the site is used exclusively for reproduction, not feeding, directly contradicting this choice. ✗
That matches Choice A — M. robustus leave the Octopus Garden upon reaching an intermediary stage of development..
